findings.dominantFinding: The dominant imaging finding is a hyperdense, soft-tissue globe mass with associated nodular or stippled calcifications [1]. The mass is typically seen projecting into the vitreous chamber of an otherwise normal-sized globe [1, 2].
findings.supportingFeatures: Supporting features include avid contrast enhancement of the mass and variable degrees of retinal detachment [1, 3]. On MRI, the lesion typically demonstrates T1 hyperintensity and T2 hypointensity relative to the vitreous fluid [1].
findings.criticalNegatives: Importantly, there is no evidence of microphthalmia to suggest persistent hyperplastic primary vitreous or retinopathy of prematurity [3, 4]. Furthermore, there is no extension of the mass beyond the globe into the optic nerve or retro-orbital fat [5].
findings.influentialFeature: The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of an intraocular mass with prominent calcifications within a normal-sized globe in a child [1].

differentials.primaryDiagnosis: The appearances are most in keeping with Retinoblastoma, given the presence of a calcified globe mass in a normal-sized eye [1]. This is the most common intraocular malignancy in childhood, often presenting with leukocoria, and the presence of calcification is highly characteristic, seen in over 95% of cases [1].
differentials.principleDifferential: The principle differential would be Persistent hyperplastic primary vitreous (PHPV), however this condition characteristically presents with microphthalmia and completely lacks calcification [3, 4]. PHPV results from failed regression of the embryonic hyaloid vascular system, which can result in retinal detachment but without the solid calcified mass seen here [3].
differentials.excludeDiagnosis: A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Coats disease, given it can also present with leukocoria and retinal detachment, and misdiagnosing it could lead to inappropriate consideration of enucleation or chemotherapy. However, Coats disease is characterized by a normal-sized globe with subretinal exudate that does not calcify, distinguishing it from retinoblastoma [4].

management.priority: From a radiological perspective, the priority is to stage the disease by accurately assessing for any extraocular extension, optic nerve involvement, or intracranial spread [5].
management.priorImaging: I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is critical to assess treatment response, and reviewing family history or prior screening is relevant since 40% of cases are inherited in an autosomal dominant fashion [5, 6].
management.furtherImaging: I would recommend a dedicated MRI of the orbits and brain with contrast, as this modality is superior here for definitively demonstrating subtle optic nerve invasion, retrobulbar spread, and screening for synchronous midline intracranial tumors such as pineal or suprasellar lesions (trilateral or quadrilateral retinoblastoma) [1, 5].
management.mriSequences: I would specify high-resolution surface coil T1 and T2-weighted sequences of the orbits to assess the local tumor extent, alongside pre- and post-contrast fat-suppressed T1 sequences to evaluate optic nerve and leptomeningeal enhancement [1, 5].
management.mdtDiscussion: This should go to the Paediatric Ocular Oncology MDT. My report would clearly document the size of the tumor, the presence or absence of optic nerve invasion, and any intracranial lesions to guide treatment options such as limb-sparing systemic chemotherapy, focal laser therapy, or surgical enucleation [1, 5].

vivaAnswer.fullScript:
The dominant imaging finding is a hyperdense, soft-tissue globe mass with associated nodular or stippled calcifications [1]. The mass is typically seen projecting into the vitreous chamber of an otherwise normal-sized globe [1, 2]. Supporting features include avid contrast enhancement of the mass and variable degrees of retinal detachment [1, 3]. On MRI, the lesion typically demonstrates T1 hyperintensity and T2 hypointensity relative to the vitreous fluid [1]. Importantly, there is no evidence of microphthalmia to suggest persistent hyperplastic primary vitreous or retinopathy of prematurity [3, 4]. Furthermore, there is no extension of the mass beyond the globe into the optic nerve or retro-orbital fat [5]. The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of an intraocular mass with prominent calcifications within a normal-sized globe in a child [1].

The appearances are most in keeping with Retinoblastoma, given the presence of a calcified globe mass in a normal-sized eye [1]. This is the most common intraocular malignancy in childhood, often presenting with leukocoria, and the presence of calcification is highly characteristic, seen in over 95% of cases [1]. The principle differential would be Persistent hyperplastic primary vitreous (PHPV), however this condition characteristically presents with microphthalmia and completely lacks calcification [3, 4]. PHPV results from failed regression of the embryonic hyaloid vascular system, which can result in retinal detachment but without the solid calcified mass seen here [3]. A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Coats disease, given it can also present with leukocoria and retinal detachment, and misdiagnosing it could lead to inappropriate consideration of enucleation or chemotherapy. However, Coats disease is characterized by a normal-sized globe with subretinal exudate that does not calcify, distinguishing it from retinoblastoma [4].

From a radiological perspective, the priority is to stage the disease by accurately assessing for any extraocular extension, optic nerve involvement, or intracranial spread [5]. I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is critical to assess treatment response, and reviewing family history or prior screening is relevant since 40% of cases are inherited in an autosomal dominant fashion [5, 6]. I would recommend a dedicated MRI of the orbits and brain with contrast, as this modality is superior here for definitively demonstrating subtle optic nerve invasion, retrobulbar spread, and screening for synchronous midline intracranial tumors such as pineal or suprasellar lesions (trilateral or quadrilateral retinoblastoma) [1, 5]. I would specify high-resolution surface coil T1 and T2-weighted sequences of the orbits to assess the local tumor extent, alongside pre- and post-contrast fat-suppressed T1 sequences to evaluate optic nerve and leptomeningeal enhancement [1, 5]. This should go to the Paediatric Ocular Oncology MDT. My report would clearly document the size of the tumor, the presence or absence of optic nerve invasion, and any intracranial lesions to guide treatment options such as limb-sparing systemic chemotherapy, focal laser therapy, or surgical enucleation [1, 5].
